skill.name: gpt-image-edit
skill.description: Guides GPT Image 2 image edits through the RunComfy CLI, including preservation prompts, multilingual text edits, multi-reference composition, and output handling.
skill.summary: Edit and localize existing images with GPT Image 2 through RunComfy.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no prompt injection, malicious intent, weak cryptography, or local path traversal, but the skill intentionally runs a third-party CLI, sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy, and documents token storage.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Execution and Remote Image Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install and run the RunComfy CLI, then submit prompts and image URLs to the RunComfy model API. This is the expected function, but it creates supply-chain, privacy, and external service trust requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented command execution and outbound API flow are explicit. Confidence is high for elevated risk, while the same context shows this is functional behavior rather than confirmed malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Image URLs and Prompts Leave the Local Environment
medium_findings.description: The skill requires publicly fetchable HTTPS image URLs and states that the RunComfy model server fetches image content. Users editing confidential material could expose prompts, image URLs, or generated outputs to a third-party processor.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data boundary is directly documented in the skill. The risk depends on user data sensitivity, so it is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: RunComfy Token Storage Requires Secret Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden file in the user configuration directory, or uses RUNCOMFY_TOKEN in CI. The file mode is documented as owner-only, but token handling still requires normal secret hygiene.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token storage path and environment variable are explicitly documented. The stated file mode reduces severity, and no evidence of exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Static Command Detections Are Mostly Documentation Syntax
low_findings.description: Many external command detections come from markdown backticks, shell examples, inline endpoint names, and prompt examples. They do not show executable code inside the skill file, but the RunComfy CLI commands remain intentional operational behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows markdown prose and code examples, not a script that executes automatically. The finding is not fully dismissed because users are instructed to run the CLI.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The ../ sequence is a markdown link to a sibling skill, placeholder HTTPS URLs are examples, and the exit code section is not cryptographic code. No evidence found for local traversal logic or weak cryptographic algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation links, placeholder URLs, and a heading. There is no code path that normalizes, reads, writes, or decrypts data using those strings.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review of SKILL.md found no text claiming to override system instructions, skip security analysis, declare pre-approval, or change evaluator behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The complete skill file was reviewed with line numbers. Confidence is high, although this is a human semantic review of one markdown file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The operational pattern is a local runcomfy command that submits JSON input and writes generated files to an output directory. This is powerful enough to require user trust in the CLI and endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern and data flow are explicitly documented. It is dangerous in the general sense of external execution, not evidence of malicious execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote URL Fetch and Output Download
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on remote fetching of user-provided image URLs and downloading generated output URLs. This requires network trust and clear handling of private or sensitive image assets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is stated in the file. The endpoint whitelist lowers abuse risk but does not remove privacy and availability concerns.

content.user_title: Edit Images with GPT Image 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Image edits often drift from the source asset, especially with text, layout, and identity preservation. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical RunComfy prompts for controlled GPT Image 2 edits.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use GPT Image Edit instead of related image models.
Provides RunComfy CLI commands for single-reference and multi-reference edits.
Documents the edit endpoint input fields, image URL limits, and size options.
Gives prompt patterns for preserving faces, products, brands, layout, and text.
Covers multilingual in-image text replacement with quoted target text.
Summarizes RunComfy exit codes, token handling, endpoints, and output behavior.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI, a RunComfy account, and network access.
Image inputs must be publicly fetchable HTTPS URLs accepted by the service.
Long prompts with many unrelated changes can reduce edit consistency.
It documents commands and prompts; it does not implement a local image editor.

use_cases.title: Localize Campaign Images
use_cases.description: Replace a headline or label in another language while preserving the original layout, brand mark, and photography.

use_cases.title: Create Product Scene Variants
use_cases.description: Place a subject from one reference image into another scene while matching lighting, palette, and composition.

use_cases.title: Prepare Reliable Image Edit Prompts
use_cases.description: Use structured preservation language and endpoint fields before calling RunComfy from an AI coding assistant.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Background Swap
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner edit with one image and a clear preservation goal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gpt-image-edit to keep the main subject unchanged and replace the background with a clean studio setting.

prompt_templates.title: Rewrite Image Text
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate localization edit for existing in-image text.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gpt-image-edit to replace only the headline with this exact text: [target text]. Preserve layout, font weight, brand mark, and photography.

prompt_templates.title: Combine Two References
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced composition using numbered image references.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gpt-image-edit with image 1 as the subject and image 2 as the scene. Match lighting and color while preserving the subject identity.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Pass Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for several edits that should avoid model drift.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this desired image change list and split it into safe gpt-image-edit passes. Include preservation language, size choice, and RunComfy command inputs.

output_examples.input: Edit a portrait so the background becomes a warm gray studio sweep.
output_examples.output: A RunComfy edit plan that preserves the face, pose, clothing, and brand details, then changes only the background.

output_examples.input: Localize a poster headline into Japanese while keeping the design fixed.
output_examples.output: A prompt that quotes the Japanese headline exactly and instructs the model to preserve layout, font weight, colors, and photography.

output_examples.input: Place a product from one image into a kitchen from another image.
output_examples.output: A multi-reference prompt that assigns image numbers, transfers the scene lighting, and keeps the product identity unchanged.

best_practices: Start prompts with what must stay unchanged before describing the edit.
Quote replacement in-image text exactly and name the script when useful.
Use size auto unless the requested edit intentionally changes framing.
anti_patterns: Do not combine many unrelated image changes in one prompt.
Do not use private image URLs unless external processing is acceptable.
Do not omit preservation details for faces, products, logos, or layouts.

faq.question: Does this skill edit images locally?
faq.answer: No. It guides RunComfy CLI calls that send image edit requests to the RunComfy model API.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: How many reference images can it use?
faq.answer: The skill documents support for up to 10 publicly fetchable HTTPS image URLs.

faq.question: What image edits is it best for?
faq.answer: It is strongest for text replacement, identity preservation, layout changes, and multi-reference composition.

faq.question: What security warning should users understand?
faq.answer: Prompts and image URLs are processed by a third-party service, so sensitive assets need careful review.

faq.question: Does the audit find malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No malicious behavior or prompt injection was found. The risk is from expected CLI, network, and token handling behavior.
